name: stochastic gradient boosting
best_quote: Stochastic gradient boosting (Friedman, 1999) follows the recipe exactly.

name: error-correcting output codes
best_quote: An early example of a learning ensemble is a method designed for multi-class classification using error-correcting output codes (Dietterich and Bakiri, 1995, ECOC).

name: hamming distance
best_quote: The Hamming distance between two vectors is the number mismatches between corresponding entries.

name: bet on sparsity principle
best_quote: Use a procedure that does well in sparse problems, since no procedure does well in dense problems.

name: monotone lasso
best_quote: Hastie et al. (2007) show that this infinitesimal forward stagewise algorithm (FS0) fits a monotone version of the lasso, which optimally reduces at each step the loss function for a given increase in the arc length of the coefficient path.

name: importance sampled learning ensemble
best_quote: Friedman and Popescu (2003) propose a hybrid approach which breaks this process down into two stages: A finite dictionary TL = {T1(x), T2(x), . . . , TM(x)} of basis functions is induced from the training data; A family of functions fλ(x) is built by fitting a lasso path in this dictionary

name: characteristic width of sampling scheme
best_quote: They propose a natural measure of the characteristic width σ of the sampling scheme S, σ = ES[Q(γ) − Q(γ∗)]. σ too narrow suggests too many of the b(x; γm) look alike, and similar to b(x; γ∗); σ too wide implies a large spread in the b(x; γm), but possibly consisting of many irrelevant cases.

name: rule ensemble
best_quote: Here we describe a modification of the tree-ensemble method that focuses on individual rules (Friedman and Popescu, 2003). We encountered rules in Section 9.3 in the discussion of the PRIM method. The idea is to enlarge an ensemble of trees by constructing a set of rules from each of the trees in the collection.

name: pairwise Markov
best_quote: In a Markov graph G, the absence of an edge implies that the corresponding random variables are conditionally independent given the variables at the other vertices. This is expressed with the following notation: X ⊥ Y | rest if X and Y are not adjacent, where 'rest' refers to all of the other vertices in the graph.

name: clique potentials
best_quote: A probability density function f over a Markov graph G can be can represented as [product over cliques] where C is the set of maximal cliques, and the positive functions ψC(·) are called clique potentials. These are not in general density functions, but rather are affinities that capture the dependence in XC by scoring certain instances xC higher than others.

name: graphical lasso
best_quote: The resulting procedure is called the graphical lasso, proposed by Friedman et al. (2007a) building on the work of Banerjee et al. (2008).

name: global markov property
best_quote: Separators have the nice property that they break the graph into conditionally independent pieces. Specifically, in a Markov graph G with subgraphs A, B and C, [if C separates A and B then] A ⊥ B | C. These are known as the global Markov properties of G.

name: clique in graph theory
best_quote: A clique is a complete subgraph— a set of vertices that are all adjacent to one another; it is called maximal if it is a clique and no additional vertices can be added to it and still yield a clique.

name: gaussian graphical model
best_quote: The Gaussian distribution has the property that all conditional distributions are also Gaussian. The inverse covariance matrix Σ−1 contains information about the partial covariances between the variables; that is, the covariances between pairs i and j, conditioned on all other variables.

name: natural parameter for gaussian graphical model
best_quote: Thus Θ captures all the second-order information (both structural and quantitative) needed to describe the conditional distribution of each node given the rest, and is the so-called "natural" parameter for the Gaussian graphical model

name: covariance graph
best_quote: Another (different) kind of graphical model is the covariance graph or relevance network, in which vertices are connected by bidirectional edges if the covariance (rather than the partial covariance) between the corresponding variables is nonzero.

name: relevance network
best_quote: Another (different) kind of graphical model is the covariance graph or relevance network, in which vertices are connected by bidirectional edges if the covariance (rather than the partial covariance) between the corresponding variables is nonzero. These are popular in genomics

name: iterative proportional fitting procedure
best_quote: This is an equality-constrained convex optimization problem, and a number of methods have been proposed for solving it, in particular the iterative proportional fitting procedure (Speed and Kiiveri, 1986).

name: meinshausen-bühlmann approach
best_quote: Meinshausen and Bühlmann (2006) take a simple approach to the problem: rather than trying to fully estimate Σ or Θ = Σ−1, they only estimate which components of θij are nonzero. To do this, they fit a lasso regression using each variable as the response and the others as predictors.

name: pathwise coordinate descent for graphical lasso
best_quote: Friedman et al. (2007a) use the pathwise coordinate descent method (Section 3.8.6) to solve the modified lasso problem at each stage.

name: ising model
best_quote: Denoting the binary valued variable at node j by Xj, the Ising model for their joint probabilities is given by Pr(X) = exp[Σ(j,k)∈E θjkXjXk - Φ(Θ)] with X = {0, 1}^p.

name: visible units
best_quote: In addition, the values at each node can be observed ("visible") or unobserved ("hidden").

name: hidden units in boltzmann machines
best_quote: In addition, the values at each node can be observed ("visible") or unobserved ("hidden").

name: poisson log-linear modeling for graphical models
best_quote: Poisson log-linear modeling, where we treat the problem as a large regression problem (Exercise 17.12). The response vector y is the vector of 2p counts in each of the cells of the multiway tabulation of the data.

name: mean field approximation
best_quote: The mean field approximation (Peterson and Anderson, 1987) estimates EΘ(XjXk) by EΘ(Xj)EΘ(Xj), and replaces the input variables by their means, leading to a set of nonlinear equations for the parameters θjk.

name: recursive feature elimination
best_quote: Ad-hoc methods for feature selection have been proposed, for example, removing genes with small coefficients, and refitting the classifier. This is done in a backward stepwise manner, starting with the smallest weights and moving on to larger weights. This is known as recursive feature elimination (Guyon et al., 2002).

name: hard thresholding
best_quote: An alternative is to use hard thresholding... we prefer soft-thresholding, as it is a smoother operation and typically works better.

name: elastic net penalty
best_quote: The elastic net penalty (Zou and Hastie, 2005) is a compromise, and has the form λ[αΣ|βj| + (1/2)(1-α)Σβj^2]. The second term encourages highly correlated features to be averaged, while the first term encourages a sparse solution in the coefficients of these averaged features.

name: regularized discriminant analysis
best_quote: RDA overcomes the singularity issues by regularizing the within-covariance estimate Σ̂. Here we use a version of RDA that shrinks Σ̂ towards its diagonal: Σ̂(γ) = γ Σ̂ + (1 −γ)diag( Σ̂), with γ ∈[0, 1].

name: medoid
best_quote: Distances and inner-products also allow us to compute the medoid in each class—the observation with smallest average distance to other observations in that class.

name: junction tree algorithm
best_quote: A prominent example is the join tree or junction tree algorithm for computing marginal and low order probabilities from the joint distribution on a graph.

name: pairwise markov graph
best_quote: Here there is a potential function for each edge (pair of variables as in f (2) above), and at most second–order interactions are represented. These are more parsimonious in terms of parameters, easier to work with, and give the minimal complexity implied by the graph structure.

name: inverse covariance matrix
best_quote: The inverse covariance matrix Σ−1 contains information about the partial covariances between the variables; that is, the covariances between pairs i and j, conditioned on all other variables. In particular, if the ijth component of Θ = Σ−1 is zero, then variables i and j are conditionally independent, given the other variables

name: modified regression algorithm for graphical models
best_quote: The graph estimation problem is not p separate regression problems, but rather p coupled problems. The use of the common W in step (b), in place of the observed cross-products matrix, couples the problems together in the appropriate fashion.

name: positive definite completion
best_quote: The estimation of Σ̂ is an example of what is sometimes called the positive definite "completion" of S.

name: soft-threshold operator
best_quote: where S is the soft-threshold operator: S(x, t) = sign(x)(|x| −t)+.

name: boltzmann machine
best_quote: They are sometimes called Ising models in the statistical mechanics literature, and Boltzmann machines in the machine learning literature, where the vertices are referred to as "nodes" or "units" and are binary-valued.

name: restricted boltzmann machine
best_quote: A restricted Boltzmann machine (RBM) consists of one layer of visible units and one layer of hidden units with no connections within each layer.

name: contrastive divergence
best_quote: Hinton (2002) noticed empirically that learning still works well if we estimate the second expectation in (17.37) by starting the Markov chain at the data and only running for a few steps (instead of to convergence). He calls this contrastive divergence

name: greedy layer-by-layer learning
best_quote: The details and justification for learning features in this greedy, layer-by-layer way are described in Hinton et al. (2006).

name: high-dimensional problem
best_quote: In this chapter we discuss prediction problems in which the number of features p is much larger than the number of observations N, often written p ≫N. Such problems have become of increasing importance, especially in genomics and other areas of computational biology. We will see that high variance and overfitting are a major concern in this setting.

name: diagonal-covariance lda
best_quote: The simplest form of regularization assumes that the features are independent within each class, that is, the within-class covariance matrix is diagonal. Despite the fact that features will rarely be independent within a class, when p ≫N we don't have enough data to estimate their dependencies.

name: independence rule
best_quote: It is also called the "independence rule" in Bickel and Levina (2004), who demonstrate theoretically that it will often outperform standard linear discriminant analysis in high-dimensional problems.

name: nearest shrunken centroids
best_quote: We would like to regularize in a way that automatically drops out features that are not contributing to the class predictions. We can do this by shrinking the classwise mean toward the overall mean, for each feature separately. The result is a regularized version of the nearest centroid classifier, or equivalently a regularized version of the diagonal-covariance form of LDA. We call the procedure nearest shrunken centroids (NSC).

name: soft thresholding for nearest shrunken centroids
best_quote: We shrink the dkj toward zero using soft thresholding d'kj = sign(dkj)(|dkj| −Δ)+

name: symmetric multiclass logistic model
best_quote: With K classes, we use a symmetric version of the multiclass logistic model (4.17) on page 119: Pr(G = k|X = x) = exp(βk0 + x^Tβk)/Σ exp(βl0 + x^Tβl)

name: one versus one approach
best_quote: In the "one versus one" (ovo) approach, we compute all pairwise classifiers. For each test point, the predicted class is the one that wins the most pairwise contests.

name: one versus all approach
best_quote: In the "one versus all" (ova) approach, each class is compared to all of the others in K two-class comparisons. To classify a test point, we compute the confidences (signed distance from the hyperplane) for each of the K classifiers. The winner is the class with the highest confidence.

name: computational shortcuts for p >> n
best_quote: When p > N, the computations can be carried out in an N-dimensional space, rather than p, via the singular value decomposition introduced in Section 14.5... This trick reduces the computational cost from O(p3) to O(pN 2) when p > N.

name: protein mass spectrometry
best_quote: Protein mass spectrometry has become a popular technology for analyzing the proteins in blood, and can be used to diagnose a disease or understand the processes underlying it. For each blood serum sample i, we observe the intensity xij for many time of flight values tj.

name: fused lasso
best_quote: The fused lasso (Tibshirani et al., 2005) solves min_β Σ(yi - Σβjxij)^2 + λ1Σ|βj| + λ2Σ|βj - βj-1|. The first penalty encourages the solution to be sparse, while the second encourages it to be smooth in the index j.

name: fused lasso signal approximator
best_quote: A particularly useful special case arises when the predictor matrix X = IN, the N × N identity matrix. This is a special case of the fused lasso, used to approximate a sequence {yi}^N_1.

name: string kernel
best_quote: Using this, we define the inner product K(x1,x2) = Σ_{a∈A^m} φ_a(x1)φ_a(x2) which measures the similarity between the two strings x1, x2.

name: bag of words representation
best_quote: We extracted all unique words from these abstracts, and defined features xij to be the number of times word j appears in abstract i. This is the so-called bag of words representation.

name: double-centered kernel
best_quote: Thus we compute the eigenvectors of the double-centered kernel (I −M)K(I −M) for the principal components from an uncentered inner-product matrix.

name: supervised principal components
best_quote: Supervised principal components is a simple approach to regression and generalized regression that is especially useful when p ≫N. We restrict attention to features which by themselves have a sizable correlation with the outcome.

name: cox proportional hazards model
best_quote: For survival problems, Cox's proportional hazards regression model is widely used; hence we use the score test from this model in step (1) and the multivariate Cox model in step (2b).

name: right censored survival data
best_quote: We have not yet discussed regression with censored survival data in this book; it represents a generalized form of regression in which the outcome variable (survival time) is only partly observed for some individuals... This individual is said to be "right censored" at 365 days.

name: kaplan-meier method
best_quote: Figure 18.12 shows the survival curve estimated by the Kaplan–Meier method for the 80 patients in the test set.

name: latent variable model for supervised pca
best_quote: Suppose we have a response variable Y which is related to an underlying latent variable U by a linear model Y = β20 + β21U + ε. In addition, we have measurements on a set of features Xj indexed by j ∈P (for pathway), for which Xj = β10j + β11jU + ϵj.

name: thresholded partial least squares
best_quote: We select the features as in steps (1) and (2a) of supervised principal components, but then apply PLS (rather than principal components) to these features. For our current discussion, we call this "thresholded PLS."

name: pre-conditioning for interpretability
best_quote: Then for interpretational purposes, as well as for practical uses, we would like some way of finding a reduced a set of features that approximates the model. Pre-conditioning (Section 18.6.3) is one way of doing this.

name: pre-conditioned lasso
best_quote: In this approach, one first computes the supervised principal component predictor ŷi for each observation in the training set (with the threshold selected by cross-validation). Then we apply the lasso with ŷi as the outcome variable, in place of the usual outcome yi.

name: two-sample t-statistic for genes
best_quote: To identify informative genes, we construct a two-sample t-statistic for each gene.

name: pooled within-group standard error
best_quote: The quantity sej is the pooled within-group standard error for gene j:

name: permutation distribution for hypothesis testing
best_quote: An attractive alternative approach is to use the permutation distribution, since it avoids assumptions about the distribution of the data. We compute (in principle) all K = (58 choose 14) permutations of the sample labels, and for each permutation k compute the t-statistics tk_j.

name: pooled permutation p-values
best_quote: To exploit the fact that the genes are similar (e.g., measured on the same scale), we can instead pool the results for all genes in computing the p-values.

name: family-wise error rate
best_quote: The family-wise error rate (FWER) is the probability of at least one false rejection, and is a commonly used overall measure of error.

name: bonferroni method
best_quote: One of the simplest approaches to multiple testing is the Bonferroni method. It makes each individual test more stringent, in order to make the FWER equal to at most α: we reject H0j if pj < α/M.

name: false discovery rate
best_quote: In the microarray setting, this is the expected proportion of genes that are incorrectly called significant, among the R genes that are called significant.

name: benjamini-hochberg procedure
best_quote: Benjamini and Hochberg (1995) first proposed the notion of false discovery rate, and gave a testing procedure (Algorithm 18.2) whose FDR is bounded by a user-defined level α.

name: plug-in estimate of false discovery rate
best_quote: The total number of permutation values |tk_j| equal or greater than 4.101 is 1518, for an average of 1518/1000 = 1.518 per permutation. Thus a direct estimate of the false discovery rate is FDR̂ = 1.518/11 ≈ 14%.
formula: \widehat{\text{FDR}} = \frac{\text{# permutation values} \geq C}{\text{# observed values} \geq C}

name: significance analysis of microarrays
best_quote: The significance analysis of microarrays (SAM) approach offers a way of doing this. The basis of the SAM method is shown in Figure 18.20. On the vertical axis we have plotted the ordered test statistics t(1) ≤ t(2) ≤ ... ≤ t(M), while the horizontal axis shows the expected order statistics from the permutations of the data.

name: asymmetric cutpoints in multiple testing
best_quote: In the testing methods described above, we used the absolute value of the test statistic tj, and hence applied the same cut-points to both positive and negative values of the statistic. In some experiments, it might happen that most or all of the differentially expressed genes change in the positive direction (or all in the negative direction). For this situation it is advantageous to derive separate cut-points for the two cases.

name: positive false discovery rate
best_quote: First we need to define the positive false discovery rate (pFDR) as

name: mixture model for hypothesis testing
best_quote: We define a random variable Zj which equals 0 if the jth null hypothesis is true, and 1 otherwise. We assume that each pair (tj, Zj) are i.i.d random variables with

name: q-value
best_quote: The q-value (Storey, 2003) of a test statistic tj is defined to be the smallest FDR over all rejection regions that reject tj. That is, for symmetric rejection regions, the q-value for tj = 2 is defined to be the FDR for the rejection region Γ = {-(∞, -2) ∪ (2, ∞)}.

name: local false discovery rate
best_quote: The local false discovery rate (Efron and Tibshirani, 2002) at t = t0 is defined to be

name: supervised principal components for feature selection
best_quote: Supervised principal components can yield lower test errors than competing methods, as shown in Figure 18.16. However, it does not always produce a sparse model involving only a small number of features (genes).

name: denoising the outcome variable
best_quote: The idea is that by first denoising the outcome variable, the lasso should not be as adversely affected by the large number of noise features.

name: feature assessment without prediction models
best_quote: Thus our goal is to assess the significance of individual features. This assessment is usually done without the use of a multivariate predictive model like those in the first part of this chapter.

name: type-I error rate
best_quote: This test has type-I error equal to α; that is, the probability of falsely rejecting H0j is α.

name: positive dependence between tests
best_quote: On the other hand, if the tests have positive dependence, that is Pr(Aj|Ak) > Pr(Aj), then the FWER will be less than (1 - (1 - α)^M). Positive dependence between tests often occurs in practice, in particular in genomic studies.

name: bh rejection threshold
best_quote: Reject all hypotheses H0j for which pj ≤ p(L), the BH rejection threshold.

name: expected order statistics from permutations
best_quote: On the vertical axis we have plotted the ordered test statistics t(1) ≤ t(2) ≤ ... ≤ t(M), while the horizontal axis shows the expected order statistics from the permutations of the data: ť(j) = (1/K) ΣK k=1 tk(j)

name: sam tuning parameter delta
best_quote: Two lines are drawn, parallel to the 45° line, Δ units away. Starting at the origin and moving to the right, we find the first place that the genes leave the band. This defines the upper cutpoint Chi and all genes beyond that point are called significant.

name: bayesian interpretation of fdr
best_quote: Hence under the mixture model (18.51), the pFDR is the posterior probability that the null hypothesis it true, given that test statistic falls in the rejection region for the test; that is, given that we reject the null hypothesis

name: radiation sensitivity gene expression study
best_quote: Consider, for example, the microarray data in Table 18.4, taken from a study on the sensitivity of cancer patients to ionizing radiation treatment (Rieger et al., 2004). Each row consists of the expression of genes in 58 patient samples: 44 samples were from patients with a normal reaction, and 14 from patients who had a severe reaction to radiation.

name: diagonal linear discriminant analysis for high dimensions
best_quote: Levina (2002) does some mathematical analysis comparing diagonal LDA to full LDA, as p, N → ∞ with p > N. She shows that with reasonable assumptions diagonal LDA has a lower asymptotic error rate than full LDA.

name: nearest shrunken centroids classifier
best_quote: Tibshirani et al. (2001a) and Tibshirani et al. (2003) proposed the nearest shrunken-centroid classifier.

name: elastic net
best_quote: while Zou and Hastie (2005) introduced the elastic net.
